romania.title: 😂 Un bărbat prins în pat cu soţia vecinului dă vina pe blocajul de la Cadastru: Credeam că e casa mea!
romania.summary: Haosul de la Agenţia Naţională de Cadastru continuă, şi o sumedenie de români au probleme în viaţa personală din cauza asta! Printre aceştia se numără Eugeniu, un bărbat de 39 de ani din Urziceni, prins recent în patul unui vecin, unde întreţinea relaţii intime cu soţia acestuia. După ce şi-a dat seama de gafă şi […] The post Un bărbat prins în pat cu soţia vecinului dă vina pe blocajul de la Cadastru: Credeam că e casa mea! appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: 😂 Nolan: Filmul „Odiseea”, bazat pe povestea reală a unui om care locuieşte în Rahova şi lucrează în Pipera
romania.summary: Regizorul şi scenaristul Christopher Nolan a confirmat aseară toate bănuielile românilor în ceea ce priveşte ultimul său blockbuster, „Odiseea”! Acesta a recunoscut că filmul e bazat pe povestea reală a lui Codruţ, un prieten al lui Nolan care locuia în Rahova şi mergea la muncă tocmai în Pipera. „Am încercat să transpunem în imagini o […] The post Nolan: Filmul „Odiseea”, bazat pe povestea reală a unui om care locuieşte în Rahova şi lucrează în Pipera appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: 😂 S-a aranjat pe viaţă! Hackerul care a spart Cadastrul şi-a trecut în proprietate o garsonieră la Cluj
romania.summary: Se pare că i-a ieşit combinaţia! Surse din zona de securitate cibernetică spun că temutul hacker care a atacat infrastructura IT a Agenţiei Naţionale de Cadastru a reuşit să îşi treacă în proprietate o locuinţă de peste 11 metri pătraţi într-o zonă destul de umană a municipiului Cluj. „Având acces la baza de date a […] The post S-a aranjat pe viaţă! Hackerul care a spart Cadastrul şi-a trecut în proprietate o garsonieră la Cluj appeared first on Times New Roman .

medical.title: How systemic immune regulation is 'hijacked' during tumor progression
medical.summary: The immune system plays a critical role in protecting the body from cancer by recognizing and eliminating abnormal cells before they can develop into tumors. This process relies on T cells that can recognize tumor-derived antigens presented by dendritic cells (DCs). However, tumor cells frequently evade immune surveillance and establish immune tolerance through various mechanisms, most of which are thought to occur within the tumor microenvironment.

medical.title: RNA testing uncovers 171 mutations driving MET exon 14 skipping in lung cancer
medical.summary: New research has shown that RNA-based testing can improve the detection and interpretation of clinically actionable genomic alterations that lead to MET exon 14 skipping in patients with lung cancer—important biomarkers that may be missed by DNA-only approaches. The findings from the study published in The Journal of Molecular Diagnostics are poised to improve diagnostic accuracy and help determine patients' eligibility for MET-targeted therapies.

medical.title: Parents' socioeconomic status is more important than prenatal behaviors to a future child's health, study suggests
medical.summary: Family socioeconomic position may be a more important determinant of children's health than parental behaviors such as smoking, drinking or caffeine consumption, according to a study published in PLOS Medicine by Gemma Sharp from the University of Exeter, UK, and colleagues.

medical.title: Why air travel can be so hard on the body
medical.summary: At any given time, hundreds of thousands of people are traveling by air. For many, these journeys will pass without consequence. But for some, a routine flight may trigger a health emergency.

medical.title: Study reveals stress a significant factor in women experiencing long COVID
medical.summary: Stress appears to play a role in women with young children who are diagnosed with post-acute sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 infection, known as Long COVID, and those who report having Long COVID symptoms. This finding, based on results from 2,500 U.S. women who completed an online survey in July 2023, is reported with related findings in an article published in Health Psychology.

medical.title: Aspirin reverses diet-driven depression-like behavior in mice
medical.summary: Depression is among the most common psychiatric disorders, estimated to affect between 280 million and 332 million people worldwide. This disorder is characterized by persistent sadness and hopelessness, low energy, a loss of interest in everyday activities and sometimes changes in appetite or sleep.

medical.title: Traveling waves in rat brains give hints about information transfer in consciousness
medical.summary: How and why we experience consciousness is a question that has long plagued philosophers and scientists alike. We have come to understand that, when awake, our brains organize neural information for perception, yet we completely lose this organization under anesthesia. Why this happens is a longstanding mystery in neuroscience, as simple changes in the activity levels of specific brain regions fail to explain this disappearance of consciousness.

medical.title: Replacing gas stoves with electric stoves greatly reduces asthma symptoms
medical.summary: More than one-third of American households rely on natural gas stoves to prepare their meals.

medical.title: Cerebral small vessel disease: New treatment possibilities for the little‑known condition affecting millions of people
medical.summary: Cerebral small vessel disease (CSVD), which notably increases the risk of stroke and cognitive disorders, is estimated to affect more than 5 million people in France alone. In the absence of a curative treatment, preventing cardiovascular risk factors remains the most effective strategy today. However, promising new therapeutic avenues are being explored.

medical.title: Brain cancer map uncovers thousands of hidden targets for personalized immunotherapies
medical.summary: Researchers from the Clinical Neuroscience Consortium (CNC), a collaborative platform jointly established by the LKS Faculty of Medicine of the University of Hong Kong (HKUMed) and Queen Mary Hospital, in collaboration with the Hong Kong Genome Institute (HKGI), have developed the most comprehensive map to date of isoform diversity in glioblastoma, the most common and aggressive form of brain cancer in adults.

science.title: New physics-based metric improves estimates of climate-driven cooling demand
science.summary: New research led by atmospheric scientists at the University of Hawai'i (UH) at Mānoa found that the century-old yardstick used across many industries to estimate air-conditioning and refrigeration energy demand—called cooling degree days—is missing something fundamental.

science.title: Melts in your mouth, but not in your car: The ongoing scientific quest to create heat‑resistant chocolate
science.summary: There are few pleasures greater than letting a piece of chocolate melt slowly on your tongue. Indeed, the proximity of chocolate's melting point to our own body temperature is one reason it is so widely appreciated.

science.title: First 3D nanoscale maps of human airway epithelium reveal hidden architecture of lung defense cells
science.summary: Researchers at Queen Mary University of London and University of Cambridge in collaboration with colleagues at HHMI Janelia and NIH, have created some of the most detailed three-dimensional maps ever generated of human airway cells, revealing how the specialized cells that protect our lungs are built and organized.

science.title: Lithium ions sprint up to 10,000 times faster when their molecular cages briefly open
science.summary: An international collaborative research group led by Professor Bong June Sung of the Department of Chemistry at Sogang University and Professor Shinji Saito of the Institute for Molecular Science (IMS), National Institutes of Natural Sciences (NINS), and the Graduate University for Advanced Studies, SOKENDAI, has elucidated, at the molecular level, how lithium ions move within organic ionic plastic crystals (OIPCs)—which are attracting attention as solid electrolytes for next-generation batteries.

science.title: Neutral catalysts achieve chalcogen-bonding asymmetry
science.summary: Chemists have demonstrated that neutral chalcogen-bond donors can induce asymmetry in chemical reactions, addressing a challenge that has limited the development of chalcogen-bonding catalysis.

science.title: Zebrafish rebuild damaged 'second brain' networks in nine days, offering clues for nerve repair
science.summary: Why can zebrafish regenerate damaged gut nerves while humans largely cannot? Michigan State University researchers have developed a new research tool that allows them to remove gut neurons in zebrafish and watch them regenerate in real time, helping scientists investigate one of regenerative biology's biggest questions. The work is published in the journal Stem Cell Reports.

science.title: Yeast survives after all 300 known spliceosomal introns are removed
science.summary: Spliceosomal introns are noncoding DNA sequences located between exons in many eukaryotic protein-coding genes. Most are removed post-transcriptionally by RNA splicing and do not encode proteins, but they have been implicated in various regulatory processes, including gene transcription, mRNA nuclear export and the generation of protein diversity. Whether spliceosomal introns are essential for fundamental cellular life has long been unclear.

science.title: Trace DNA reveals critically endangered Chinese bahaba in Hong Kong after decade without records
science.summary: The Chinese bahaba (Bahaba taipingensis), a marine fish species endemic to the Chinese coast and known as the "Panda of the Sea," is critically endangered and one of the few marine fish listed as a first-class protected wild animal in China. Collaborative research led by the Division of Science at Lingnan University has developed a cost-effective environmental DNA (eDNA) monitoring qPCR approach specifically designed for the Chinese bahaba.

science.title: Look for NASA+ Now Streaming on Amazon Fire TV
science.summary: Continuing agency efforts to bring space closer to home, NASA+ is heading to more streaming platforms. On Thursday, NASA announced its programming is on Fire TV Channels. Fire TV customers can easily access this content by asking Alexa+ on compatible devices. Future programming on Fire TV may include science mission launches, a test flight for […]

science.title: Nanopores can activate human T cells without biochemical signals
science.summary: Immune cells protect and heal the human body. In medicine, they are specifically activated through biochemical reactions to treat certain diseases. Researchers at the Helmholtz-Zentrum Hereon, ETH Zurich, Humboldt University of Berlin, Charité Berlin and Inselspital Bern have now discovered that immune cells also respond to the surface structure of materials—without chemistry.

science.title: How AI is helping states cut through decades of red tape
science.summary: Bureaucracy moves slowly, and decades of outdated provisions clogging the bylaws of cities and states only make it worse. Sometimes dubbed "policy sludge," such obsolete processes and stale reporting requirements can trap civil servants in red tape and cause program dysfunction.

science.title: Less cannabis use among adolescents, but social disparities persist
science.summary: Past studies have shown that cannabis use during adolescence is associated with a variety of negative impacts—including emotional distress, adverse academic outcomes, and brain development and functioning—that can persist into adulthood. Though there has been an overall decrease in cannabis use among Minnesota adolescents over the past decade, prior studies have been limited in their ability to examine the nuances in this trend—particularly differences in use among social groups.

science.title: Virtual café experiment finds Japanese participants keep greater distance than Italians
science.summary: How close do we stand to other people during a social interaction? The distance we maintain between our body and that of another person—known as interpersonal distance—is a fundamental aspect of everyday life. It influences our sense of comfort, safety, intimacy and tendency to approach or avoid others. This distance can vary depending on the context, the characteristics of the other person, our emotional state, and our personal and social background.

science.title: Quantum Newton's cradle set to level up computing
science.summary: Sending quantum information through a chain of qubits, like energy through a Newton's cradle, could be the key to faster operations and take quantum computing to the next level.

science.title: Recycling does not weaken support for broader climate action, survey finds
science.summary: In research published in npj Climate Action, UNSW Sydney behavioral scientists, in collaboration with researchers from Griffith University, say they found no evidence for claims that climate action at the individual level distracted from support for meaningful action on climate change.

science.title: Crews Move Artemis IV Liquid Hydrogen Tank
science.summary: Crews at NASA’s Michoud Assembly Facility in New Orleans transport the 130-foot-tall Artemis IV liquid hydrogen tank out of a production cell inside the main factory building into a detached test building on a separate portion of the 829-acre site on May 15, 2026. The liquid hydrogen tank will form part of the core stage for […]

science.title: NASA’s MAVEN Illuminates New Understanding of Auroras at Mars
science.summary: NASA MAVEN (Mars Atmosphere and Volatile Evolution) mission scientists have uncovered a key puzzle piece in understanding certain types of auroras on Mars, finding that they form in a similar way to Earth-based auroras. Results published Thursday in Nature Communications show the same mechanism that circulates and catapults charged particles into Earth’s atmosphere is happening at […]

environment.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
environment.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

environment.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
environment.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

environment.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
environment.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

environment.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
environment.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

environment.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
environment.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

environment.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
environment.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

environment.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
environment.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

environment.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
environment.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

joke_ro.text: Azi sunt foarte zen. N-am energie nici sa ma stresez.

satire_ro.title: S-a molipsit? De când s-a pupat cu un urmărit internaţional, Ciolacu dă şi el semne de urmărire internaţională
